sections.title: Hamzat al-Waṣl vs. Hamzat al-Qaṭ'
sections.explanation: Arabic has two types of hamza at the beginning of words. (1) Hamzat al-qaṭ' (همزة القطع, ءَ/أَ/إِ): a 'real' hamza that is ALWAYS pronounced, whether the word comes at the beginning of speech or in the middle. It is written with a hamza sign (ء) on or under the alif. Examples: أَكَلَ (he ate), إِسْلَام (Islam), أُمّ (mother). (2) Hamzat al-waṣl (همزة الوصل, اِ): a 'connecting' hamza that is pronounced ONLY at the start of speech. When the word comes after another word in connected speech, the hamzat al-waṣl is silent and the preceding word links directly to the next consonant. It is written as a plain alif (ا) without a hamza sign. Examples: اِسْتَمَعَ (he listened — pronounced 'istama'a' at start, but 'stama'a' after a vowel).
sections.tip: Test: say the word after وَ (and). If the initial vowel disappears in connected speech, it is hamzat al-waṣl. If it stays, it is hamzat al-qaṭ'. Example: وَأَكَلَ (wa-akala, hamza stays = qaṭ') vs. وَاسْتَمَعَ (wa-stama'a, hamza drops = waṣl).

examples.en: The boy ate (أَ = hamzat al-qaṭ', always pronounced)
The student listened (اِ = hamzat al-waṣl, drops in connected speech)
Indeed, God is generous (إِ = hamzat al-qaṭ')
the book (الـ begins with hamzat al-waṣl)

exercises.prompt: What is the difference between hamzat al-waṣl and hamzat al-qaṭ'?
options: Hamzat al-waṣl is always pronounced; hamzat al-qaṭ' is sometimes silent
Hamzat al-qaṭ' is always pronounced; hamzat al-waṣl is silent in connected speech
They are the same thing with different names
exercises.explanation: Hamzat al-qaṭ' (cutting hamza) is always pronounced. Hamzat al-waṣl (connecting hamza) is only pronounced at the start of speech and drops when preceded by another word.

exercises.prompt: Which of these words starts with hamzat al-waṣl?
exercises.explanation: اِنْطَلَقَ (Form VII verb) starts with hamzat al-waṣl. Verb Forms VII, VIII, IX, X all begin with hamzat al-waṣl. أَخَذَ and أُمٌّ have hamzat al-qaṭ'.

sections.title: Where Each Hamza Type Appears
sections.explanation: Hamzat al-waṣl appears in specific, predictable places: (1) The definite article الـ. (2) The imperative of Form I verbs: اُكْتُبْ, اِجْلِسْ. (3) The past tense, imperative, and masdar of Forms VII (اِنْفَعَلَ), VIII (اِفْتَعَلَ), IX (اِفْعَلَّ), and X (اِسْتَفْعَلَ). (4) A small number of nouns: اِبْنٌ (son), اِبْنَةٌ (daughter), اِسْمٌ (name), اِمْرَأَةٌ (woman), اِمْرُؤٌ (man), اِثْنَانِ (two m.), اِثْنَتَانِ (two f.). Everything else uses hamzat al-qaṭ': all Form I past tense verbs starting with hamza (أَكَلَ, أَخَذَ), all Form IV verbs (أَفْعَلَ), particles (إِنَّ, أَنَّ, إِلَى, إِذَا), and most nouns (أَبٌ, أُمٌّ, أُسْتَاذٌ).
sections.tip: If you are unsure, try the وَ test. Say the word after وَ — if the initial vowel disappears, it is waṣl. In writing: hamzat al-qaṭ' has ء on or under the alif (أ/إ), while hamzat al-waṣl is a bare alif (ا) — sometimes marked with a small ص above it (ٱ) in Quranic text.

examples.en: He sent a message (أَرْسَلَ = Form IV, hamzat al-qaṭ')
He used the computer (اِسْتَخْدَمَ = Form X, hamzat al-waṣl)
to the school (إِلَى = particle, hamzat al-qaṭ')

exercises.prompt: Form IV verbs (أَفْعَلَ) start with which type of hamza?
exercises.explanation: Form IV verbs (أَفْعَلَ, e.g., أَرْسَلَ) always start with hamzat al-qaṭ'. This distinguishes them from Forms VII-X which use hamzat al-waṣl.

exercises.prompt: The word for 'name' in Arabic is ___ (with hamzat al-waṣl)
exercises.explanation: اِسْمٌ is one of the small group of nouns that begin with hamzat al-waṣl. It is connected in speech: مَا اسْمُكَ (what is your name?) — the hamza drops.

sections.title: Hamza in the Middle of a Word (الهمزة المتوسّطة)
sections.explanation: When hamza appears in the middle of a word, its 'seat' (the letter it sits on) depends on the strongest vowel surrounding it. The vowel strength hierarchy is: kasra (ـِـ) is the strongest, then ḍamma (ـُـ), then fatḥa (ـَـ), and sukun (ـْـ) is the weakest. Compare the vowel ON the hamza and the vowel BEFORE it — the stronger vowel determines the seat. If kasra wins → hamza sits on yā' (ئـ). If ḍamma wins → hamza sits on wāw (ؤ). If fatḥa wins → hamza sits on alif (أ). Examples: سُئِلَ (su'ila, 'he was asked') — kasra is strongest → seat is yā'. رُؤُوسٌ (ru'ūs, 'heads') — ḍamma is strongest → seat is wāw. سَأَلَ (sa'ala, 'he asked') — fatḥa is strongest → seat is alif.
sections.tip: Remember the hierarchy: kasra > ḍamma > fatḥa > sukun. The strongest vowel always wins. Think of kasra as ي (yā'), ḍamma as و (wāw), and fatḥa as ا (alif) — each vowel 'wants' its corresponding letter as the seat.

examples.en: he asked (fatḥa + fatḥa → seat on alif: أ)
he was asked (ḍamma + kasra → kasra wins, seat on yā': ئ)
heads (ḍamma + ḍamma → seat on wāw: ؤ)
responsible (sukun + ḍamma → ḍamma wins, seat on wāw: ؤ)

exercises.prompt: In the vowel strength hierarchy for hamza seating, which vowel is strongest?
exercises.explanation: Kasra is the strongest vowel: kasra > ḍamma > fatḥa > sukun. When kasra is involved, hamza sits on yā' (ئـ).

exercises.prompt: Why is hamza written on yā' in سُئِلَ?
options: Because the vowel before hamza is ḍamma
Because the vowel on hamza is kasra, and kasra is the strongest
Because the word is in the passive voice
exercises.explanation: We compare the vowel before hamza (ḍamma on سُ) with the vowel on hamza (kasra). Kasra is stronger than ḍamma, so hamza sits on yā': سُئِلَ.
In سُؤَالٌ, we compare ḍamma (before hamza) and fatḥa (on hamza). Ḍamma is stronger, so hamza sits on wāw: سُؤَالٌ.

sections.title: Hamza at the End of a Word (الهمزة المتطرّفة)
sections.explanation: When hamza is the last letter of a word, its seat depends ONLY on the vowel of the letter before it. If the letter before has kasra → hamza on yā' (ئ): شَاطِئٌ (shāṭi', 'beach'). If the letter before has ḍamma → hamza on wāw (ؤ): تَبَاطُؤٌ (tabāṭu', 'slowness'). If the letter before has fatḥa → hamza on alif (أ): قَرَأَ (qara'a, 'he read'). If the letter before has sukun → hamza on the line (ء): شَيْءٌ (shay', 'thing'), جُزْءٌ (juz', 'part'). When tanwīn fatḥatan is added to a final hamza, the spelling may change — for example, شَيْئًا (a thing, accusative) gets an alif added.
sections.tip: Final hamza is simpler than middle hamza — you only look at ONE vowel (the letter before). Sukun = hamza sits alone on the line (ء).

examples.en: he read (fatḥa before → hamza on alif: أ)
beach (kasra before → hamza on yā': ئ)
thing (sukun before → hamza on the line: ء)
part (sukun before → hamza on the line: ء)

exercises.prompt: In the word شَيْءٌ (thing), why is hamza written on the line?
options: Because the vowel before hamza is fatḥa
Because the vowel before hamza is sukun (yā' sākina)
exercises.explanation: The yā' before hamza has sukun (شَيْـ). When the preceding letter has sukun, final hamza sits alone on the line: شَيْءٌ.
The verb 'he read' is قَرَأَ. The letter before final hamza (rā') has fatḥa, so hamza sits on alif.
